subsystem: OpenSC PKCS#15 emulation layer: sc_pkcs15_decode_prkdf_entry(), the decoder for a single Private Key Directory File (PrKDF) entry. It builds an ASN.1 template describing an SC_PKCS15_TYPE_PRKEY choice (RSA / EC / DSA / GOSTR3410), decodes an attacker-supplied DER blob read from a smart card into a struct sc_pkcs15_prkey_info, resolves the key file path, back-fills the auth_id from AccessControlRules, and finally heap-copies the populated info into obj->data.
provenance: Identified from the OpenSC API surface: the sc_pkcs15_* / sc_asn1_* / sc_context_t / u8 typedefs, the sc_format_asn1_entry / sc_copy_asn1_entry / sc_asn1_decode_choice ASN.1 template machinery, the LOG_TEST_RET / LOG_FUNC_RETURN diagnostic macros, and the GOSTR3410 keyinfo handling are all distinctive to OpenSC's src/libopensc/pkcs15-prkey.c. The banner 'primevul_c_train_3176_fixed' and the in-code OpenSC-version comment (lines 144-145) confirm the provenance; this is the post-fix revision of the routine.
trust_boundary: The hostile input is the DER/BER byte stream passed as *buf with length *buflen (parameters at line 4) and consumed by sc_asn1_decode_choice at line 92. Its bytes originate from the token/card, which is untrusted at the wire level: a malicious or malformed card can return arbitrary TLV structure, arbitrary lengths, and can cause the decoder to allocate (info.subject.value, info.params.data) or to fail partway. p15card, obj, and the surrounding handles (p15card->card->ctx, p15card->app, p15card->file_app) come from the caller and are a separate, host-side contract. The struct fields the template writes into (info.usage, info.access_flags, info.algo_refs[], info.subject.value/len, info.path, info.modulus_length) are all attacker-influenced once decode succeeds.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Entry point. It receives the card handle p15card, the target object obj to populate, and a by-reference cursor (buf, buflen) into the DER stream. buf/buflen are both in and out: sc_asn1_decode_choice advances them past the consumed entry so the caller can iterate the directory file. The (const u8 **) double pointer and size_t* are the sole channel through which untrusted card bytes enter this function.

Locals. ctx is dereferenced two levels deep from p15card without a NULL check (a caller contract). info is the stack accumulator that will hold every decoded field. gostr3410_params[3] is a fixed 3-int scratch buffer for the GOST OID triplet. usage_len and af_len capture the destination sizes of the fixed info.usage and info.access_flags bitfields and are passed by reference to the decoder as in/out length bounds, so the ASN.1 layer will not write more than sizeof(info.usage)/sizeof(info.access_flags) bytes into those members.

A family of fixed-size stack arrays of struct sc_asn1_entry, one per sub-template (common key attrs, common private-key attrs, and per-algorithm RSA/DSA/GOST/ECC key and private-key attribute lists), plus the top-level asn1_prkey choice and the supported-algorithms list. Each array is sized by a C_ASN1_*_SIZE compile-time macro that must exactly equal the number of entries in the corresponding c_asn1_* template being copied over it (line 31 onward). These arrays are decode scaffolding; each entry carries a name, a type tag, and a pointer to the destination field.

Four wrapper objects, one per key algorithm, each binding the shared obj plus the common-key, common-private-key, and algorithm-specific attribute templates. These are the payloads registered as the four alternatives of the top-level PRKEY choice so that whichever alternative the card presents, the decoder knows which sub-template and destination fields to use.

Each stack template array is initialised by memcpy-style copy from a corresponding static c_asn1_* prototype. sc_copy_asn1_entry copies entries up to and including the terminating null-name sentinel; correctness depends on every destination array (sized by its C_ASN1_*_SIZE macro at lines 12-25) being at least as large as the source prototype. A macro that under-sizes a destination would let this copy overrun the stack array — a build-time invariant the header contract must uphold.

sc_format_asn1_entry binds each template slot to its runtime destination. Lines 48-51 wire the four PRKEY choice alternatives (indices 0..3) to the wrapper objects — note the ordering RSA=0, EC=1, DSA=2, GOST=3, which is relied on later at lines 100-113. Lines 53-56 nest the per-algorithm key-attribute sub-templates inside their private-key attribute lists.

Per-algorithm decode targets. RSA fills info.path and info.modulus_length; DSA nests a value template pointing at info.path plus an indirect-protected path sub-template; GOST fills info.path and the three gostr3410_params scalars; ECC fills info.path and info.field_length. The three GOST slots write into the bounded local gostr3410_params[3] array, and the loop later assumes exactly three entries are wired.

Common key attributes: id, usage (bounded by usage_len), native flag, access_flags (bounded by af_len), and key_reference. Lines 80-81 iterate the supported-algorithms template, binding each present entry to info.algo_refs[i]; the loop is capped by SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS and by the template's null-name sentinel, then line 82 nests the algorithms list under common-key slot 5. This loop is the one place a fixed destination array (info.algo_refs) is indexed by a counter, so the relationship between SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS, C_ASN1_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS_SIZE, and the size of info.algo_refs is the key invariant.

The common-private-key template's slot 0 is bound to info.subject.value / info.subject.len — a variable-length OCTET STRING destination, so the decoder will malloc info.subject.value and set info.subject.len at decode time. Immediately after wiring, info is zeroed (this is safe because sc_format_asn1_entry stored pointers to the fields, not their contents), then defaults are applied: key_reference=-1, native=1, and gostr3410_params cleared. After this block info.subject.value is NULL until the decoder runs.

The actual untrusted parse: sc_asn1_decode_choice walks *buf/*buflen against the asn1_prkey choice, populating info and advancing the cursor. On failure (r<0) the code frees the possibly-partially-allocated info.subject.value — this free-on-error is the notable behavior of the fixed revision. If the decoder signalled end-of-contents the function returns that code; otherwise LOG_TEST_RET returns on any nonzero r. On all r<0 paths the function returns before reaching later code, so subject.value is freed at most once here.

Dispatch on which choice alternative the decoder marked SC_ASN1_PRESENT. RSA/EC/DSA just set obj->type (DSA additionally records an indirect-protected path). The GOST branch is the only one that allocates: it sets a fixed modulus_length, sets params.len to sizeof(struct sc_pkcs15_keyinfo_gostparams), malloc's params.data (NULL-checked, returning OUT_OF_MEMORY on failure), then copies the three decoded GOST OID references into the allocated struct. Several assert() calls guard the assumed pre-state (modulus_length==0, params.len==0, and struct-size equality). If no alternative is present, it logs and returns SC_ERROR_INVALID_ASN1_OBJECT.

Path resolution. If the card has no application or no DDO AID, the decoded info.path is made absolute relative to p15card->file_app->path; on failure the GOST params are freed and the error returned. Otherwise the app's AID is stamped onto the path. Then a legacy compatibility hack normalises a negative keyReference (values < -1) by adding 256, to interoperate with cards written by OpenSC <= 0.11.4. Note the error branch here frees info.params but not info.subject.value.

If the object is a private key with no auth_id set, the code scans obj->access_rules[0..SC_PKCS15_MAX_ACCESS_RULES-1] for a rule whose access_mode includes a private-key usage bit and whose auth_id is non-empty, adopting the first such auth_id. The loop is bounded by the compile-time SC_PKCS15_MAX_ACCESS_RULES and reuses index i to detect the not-found case. This operates entirely on the caller-provided obj->access_rules array, not on the freshly decoded card bytes.

Success finalisation. A heap copy of the whole info struct is allocated (NULL-checked; on failure params are freed and OUT_OF_MEMORY returned) and info is bit-copied into obj->data. This transfers ownership of the embedded heap pointers (info.subject.value and, for GOST, info.params.data) to obj->data — the caller's object-free routine is expected to release them. The two trailing sc_log calls read info.subject.value/len and info.path; those pointers are still valid (the memcpy duplicated the pointer value, it did not free the buffer). Returns 0 on success.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The decoder heap-allocates info.subject.value for the subject OCTET STRING. The early error branch (line 95) frees it, but the two LATER error branches — sc_pkcs15_make_absolute_path failure at lines 134-136 and the obj->data malloc failure at lines 178-181 — call sc_pkcs15_free_key_params(&info.params) only; they never free info.subject.value. On a card that decodes successfully but then trips either later error (e.g. an unresolvable path, or allocation pressure), the subject buffer is leaked. An attacker who can repeatedly present such entries can drive unbounded heap growth (memory-exhaustion DoS). Invariant: every non-success exit after a successful decode must release info.subject.value exactly once (or the object-cleanup that owns it must run).

what_could_go_wrong.code:
for (i=0; i<SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS && (asn1_supported_algorithms + i)->name; i++)
		sc_format_asn1_entry(asn1_supported_algorithms + i, &info.algo_refs[i], NULL, 0);
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This loop indexes two fixed arrays in lockstep: it reads (asn1_supported_algorithms + i)->name (declared with C_ASN1_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS_SIZE at line 25) and writes into info.algo_refs[i]. Safety requires (a) C_ASN1_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS_SIZE >= SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS + 1 so the ->name sentinel read never runs off asn1_supported_algorithms, and (b) sizeof(info.algo_refs)/sizeof(algo_refs[0]) >= SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS so the write never overruns algo_refs. If a header change makes SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS larger than either array, this becomes an out-of-bounds read of the template names (CWE-125) and/or an out-of-bounds write into the info struct (CWE-787). The loop bound is a compile-time macro rather than an attacker length, so this is a latent header-invariant hazard, not directly card-controlled.

info.subject.value is freed but not set to NULL, and info still lives on the stack. In the current control flow every r<0 path returns before subject.value is touched again, so there is no active use-after-free or double-free. The hazard is latent and fragile: any future code inserted between line 96 and the returns (or a change that lets execution fall through with r<0) would operate on a released pointer — an operation-on-a-freed-resource (CWE-672 / CWE-416). The robust form frees and nulls (free(x); x = NULL;) so a later free or read is inert. This is the exact class the fixed revision is guarding against.

When p15card->app is NULL the guard's short-circuit takes the true branch and immediately dereferences p15card->file_app->path. If file_app is also NULL (a card/enumeration state where no application file was selected), this is a NULL-pointer dereference (CWE-476) and a crash-class DoS. The condition is partly card/state driven — a card that presents a PrKDF entry without a corresponding app context reaches this path. Invariant: file_app must be non-NULL whenever app is NULL/empty, or the code must guard file_app before dereferencing it.

These assert() calls run on state that is influenced by the decoded entry. In a build compiled with assertions enabled (NDEBUG unset), an assert whose predicate an attacker can falsify becomes a remotely reachable abort() — a denial of service (CWE-617). Here modulus_length and params.len are memset to 0 (line 87) and only the GOST branch writes them, so violating the first two asserts requires the ASN.1 template to populate modulus_length/params.len on a GOST decode, which the wiring does not do — low practical reachability. Still, using assert() rather than a graceful error return on values downstream of untrusted input is the wrong tool; the malloc NULL-check on the same lines correctly uses a return instead.

This single call is the entire trust boundary: it consumes attacker-controlled *buf/*buflen. All downstream safety (bounded copies into info.usage/access_flags via the length caps at lines 75/77, the OCTET-STRING allocation for info.subject, the numeric fields) depends on sc_asn1_decode_choice honouring those in/out length bounds and never writing past the fixed destinations. This function cannot itself enforce those bounds — it delegates them to the ASN.1 core. The residual risk is entirely in that core: a length/count field in the DER stream that the ASN.1 decoder fails to bound against a fixed destination would surface as an overflow here, with these struct fields as the sinks. Invariant to confirm: the ASN.1 layer treats usage_len/af_len as hard maxima and rejects over-long elements before writing.

summary: This is the post-fix OpenSC PrKDF-entry decoder; it is largely careful — untrusted card bytes are confined to a single sc_asn1_decode_choice call, mallocs are NULL-checked, and the notable fix frees a partially-allocated subject buffer on decode error. Residual issues are minor and mostly latent: info.subject.value is still leaked on the two later error paths (make_absolute_path failure and obj->data malloc failure), the freed subject pointer is not nulled (a fragile operation-after-release contract, CWE-672), the supported-algorithms loop relies on unshown header size invariants, and p15card->file_app is dereferenced without a NULL guard. No directly attacker-triggered memory-corruption is visible in this revision.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: sc_pkcs15_decode_prkdf_entry(..., const u8 ** buf, size_t *buflen) signature
reviewed_anchors.reason: buf/buflen carry the untrusted card DER stream — the sole attacker input channel and the parse trust boundary
two-level dereference of a caller-owned handle; NULL-safety is a documented caller contract, not card-controlled
stack accumulator whose embedded heap pointers (subject.value, params.data) drive the leak / operation-after-release contracts
fixed 3-int local exactly matched to the three GOST slots wired at lines 67-69; bounded
in/out length caps set to the true destination sizes; correctly bound the decoder's writes into the fixed usage/access_flags fields

reviewed_anchors.anchor: asn1_* stack template arrays sized by C_ASN1_*_SIZE macros (lines 12-25)
reviewed_anchors.reason: sizes are compile-time macros that must match their c_asn1_* prototypes; a build-time invariant, no runtime attacker control
static pointer bundles for the choice alternatives; no length or buffer semantics
template copies bounded by the destination array macros; safe provided the C_ASN1_*_SIZE invariants hold (covered by the array anchor)

reviewed_anchors.anchor: sc_format_asn1_entry wiring of choice + per-algorithm targets (lines 48-72)
reviewed_anchors.reason: binds pointers to fixed struct members; no per-element length bound crosses here
the only common-key slots with length caps, and both caps equal the destination sizeof — correctly bounded
counter indexes two fixed arrays; safety depends on unshown header size relationships (potential OOB read/write)
registers a heap-allocating OCTET-STRING destination whose cleanup is missing on later error paths (leak)
zeroes fields after pointer wiring; correct ordering, no security contract violated
the untrusted-parse call; all bounding of card-controlled lengths is delegated here
operation-after-release contract (CWE-672); currently safe but fragile, matches the fixed-revision guard
plain early return after the free; no resource left dangling
diagnostic macro dereferences ctx which is validated by caller contract; returns on nonzero r
index-fixed flag reads on the local template array; ordering matches the wiring at lines 48-51
assert() on values downstream of untrusted input is a reachable-abort (CWE-617) anti-pattern even though practical reachability is low
fixed-size allocation, return value checked, error returns gracefully
file_app dereferenced without a NULL guard when app is absent (potential NULL deref)
frees params but omits info.subject.value on these paths — the leak sink
legacy compatibility normalisation on a scalar; no memory or bound impact
iterates a caller-owned fixed array with a compile-time bound; index used only for the not-found check
fixed-size allocation, NULL-checked; memcpy transfers ownership of embedded pointers as designed
reads subject.value after the memcpy; the pointer was duplicated not freed, so it is still valid

findings.title: Decoded subject.value leaked on later error paths
findings.bug_class: Memory leak on error path (incomplete cleanup of heap-allocated decode output)
findings.invariant: After a successful decode allocates info.subject.value, every non-success exit must free it exactly once (mirroring the free at line 95) or hand it to the object-cleanup that owns it.
findings.failure_mode: A card that decodes cleanly (subject OCTET STRING present, so info.subject.value is malloc'd) but then fails sc_pkcs15_make_absolute_path (lines 133-136) or the obj->data allocation (lines 178-181) returns without freeing info.subject.value; only info.params is freed. Repeatedly presenting such entries leaks the subject buffer each time, enabling gradual heap exhaustion / DoS.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm info.subject.value is non-NULL after a successful decode with a subject present, and that neither the make_absolute_path error branch nor the obj->data malloc-failure branch frees it before returning.

findings.title: Freed subject pointer not nulled — fragile operation-after-release
findings.bug_class: Operation on a released resource (latent use-after-free / double-free)
findings.invariant: A pointer freed on an error path must be set to NULL (or the function must provably not touch it again) so any subsequent free or dereference is inert.
findings.failure_mode: info.subject.value is freed but left dangling in the still-live stack struct info. Today all r<0 paths return before it is reused, so there is no active bug; but the guard is fragile — any future statement inserted before the returns, or a control-flow change that reaches the later free/memcpy with r<0, would read or re-free the released buffer (use-after-free / double-free). This is precisely the class the fixed revision addresses; nulling after free removes the latent hazard.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify no code path uses info.subject.value after line 95 when r<0, and consider assigning info.subject.value = NULL immediately after the free.

findings.title: Supported-algorithms loop depends on unshown array-size invariants
findings.code:
for (i=0; i<SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS && (asn1_supported_algorithms + i)->name; i++)
		sc_format_asn1_entry(asn1_supported_algorithms + i, &info.algo_refs[i], NULL, 0);
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read/write via array-size macro mismatch
findings.invariant: C_ASN1_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS_SIZE must be >= SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS + 1 (for the null-name sentinel), and sizeof(info.algo_refs)/sizeof(info.algo_refs[0]) must be >= SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS.
findings.failure_mode: The loop reads (asn1_supported_algorithms + i)->name and writes info.algo_refs[i] with i running up to SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS-1. If a header revision makes SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS exceed the asn1_supported_algorithms element count, the sentinel read runs past that stack array (OOB read, CWE-125); if it exceeds the algo_refs element count, the wiring writes past info.algo_refs (OOB write into the struct, CWE-787). The bound is a compile-time macro, so this is a latent invariant to verify against the header definitions rather than a directly card-controlled overflow.
findings.what_to_confirm: Check the header definitions of SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS, C_ASN1_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS_SIZE, and the declared length of struct sc_pkcs15_prkey_info.algo_refs to confirm the two size relationships hold.

findings.title: file_app dereferenced without NULL guard when app is absent
findings.invariant: p15card->file_app must be non-NULL on every path where p15card->app is NULL or has a zero-length AID, or file_app must be checked before &p15card->file_app->path is taken.
findings.failure_mode: When p15card->app is NULL the condition short-circuits true and the code immediately forms &p15card->file_app->path. If file_app is also NULL for that card/enumeration state, this dereferences NULL and crashes (DoS). The trigger depends on card/framework state (a PrKDF entry reached without an established app file context) rather than the DER bytes directly.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm that the PKCS#15 enumeration guarantees p15card->file_app is set before sc_pkcs15_decode_prkdf_entry runs, or add an explicit NULL check on file_app.

findings.title: assert() used on values downstream of untrusted decode
findings.bug_class: Reachable assertion / abort on input-derived state
findings.invariant: Sanity conditions on state that can be influenced by decoded input should be enforced with graceful error returns (as the adjacent malloc NULL-check is), not assert(), which is compiled out under NDEBUG and aborts the process otherwise.
findings.failure_mode: In an assertions-enabled build, any predicate here that untrusted input could falsify would abort() the process — a DoS. Practical reachability is low because info.modulus_length and info.params.len are memset to 0 (line 87) and the GOST template does not populate them, so the first two asserts should hold; the third checks a static struct-size identity. The concern is the pattern (assert on input-adjacent state) plus the behavioral divergence between debug and release builds.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no ASN.1 template path writes info.modulus_length or info.params.len before the GOST branch, and consider replacing these asserts with SC_ERROR_INVALID_ASN1_OBJECT returns.

audit_checklist: Trace every non-success exit after line 92 and confirm info.subject.value is freed exactly once (fix the make_absolute_path and obj->data-malloc error branches to release it).
Set info.subject.value = NULL immediately after free(info.subject.value) at line 95 to harden against future use-after-free / double-free.
Verify the header relationships SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS < C_ASN1_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS_SIZE and SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS <= the element count of info.algo_refs.
Add a NULL check on p15card->file_app before &p15card->file_app->path (line 133), or prove the enumerator always sets it.
Confirm sc_asn1_decode_choice honours usage_len (line 75) and af_len (line 77) as hard upper bounds and rejects over-long usage/access_flags elements before writing into the fixed info fields.
Replace the assert() calls in the GOST branch (lines 114, 116, 121) with graceful SC_ERROR_* returns so behaviour is identical in debug and release builds.
Confirm the object-free routine that owns obj->data releases both info.subject.value and, for GOST, info.params.data, so the ownership transfer at line 182 does not leak.
